one_line_takeaway: A non-experimental survey that unifies the DPO/IPO/KTO/SimPO/ORPO/PPO zoo under a three-axis (preference model / regularization / data coverage) taxonomy and maps alignment failure modes like reward overoptimization and length hacking to specific design choices -- valuable as a map, but its theorems and numbers are all borrowed from ~50+ cited works.
key_finding: The central claim is predictive taxonomy: failure modes are not idiosyncratic but arise from specific, identifiable combinations of the three design axes -- length hacking from reward-model length bias plus insufficient regularization; mode collapse from overly strong KL; likelihood displacement from DPO's offline gradient asymmetry; preference collapse from missing regularization. Concrete synthesized conclusions the survey draws from cited work: (a) the coverage-separation result (Song et al. [16]) predicts DPO/IPO (offline contrastive) need GLOBAL data coverage while PPO (online) needs only PARTIAL coverage, explaining why PPO wins when preference data is sparse and DPO wins when data is diverse; (b) SimPO's consistent edge over DPO is explained by three factors -- length-normalized rewards fix verbosity bias, an explicit target margin gamma supplies regularization DPO lacks, and its reference-free design avoids reference-model miscalibration; (c) Ivison et al. [18] found data quality dominates algorithm choice (an ~8% gain from better data vs. ~2.5% from choosing PPO over DPO), which the authors read as evidence the field may be optimizing the wrong variable. Default practitioner recommendation: SimPO for most moderate-compute settings; PPO when coverage is limited or exploration is needed; DPO when data is high-quality and diverse and the reference model is well-calibrated.
core_contribution: This is a theoretical SURVEY, not an experimental paper. Its own contribution is an organizing framework that claims the proliferation of preference-learning methods (DPO, IPO, KTO, SimPO, ORPO, GRPO, PPO/RLHF) reduces to principled choices along three orthogonal axes: (I) Preference Model (Bradley-Terry, Plackett-Luce, prospect-theoretic, game-theoretic), (II) Regularization Mechanism (explicit KL, implicit KL, reference-free, f-divergences), and (III) Data Distribution (online vs. offline, coverage requirements). The authors (a) re-cast the method zoo as instantiations of Azar et al.'s general PsiPO objective (choosing Psi=-log-sigmoid recovers DPO, Psi=(z-1)^2 recovers IPO, Psi=hinge recovers SLiC-HF); (b) build a taxonomy (Table 1) mapping each method to its position on the three axes; (c) connect empirical failure modes (length hacking, mode collapse, likelihood displacement, preference collapse, overoptimization) to specific design-choice combinations (Table 2); and (d) distill a practitioner's decision guide (Table 4). The framing sentence is that the work 'transforms preference learning from an empirical art into a theoretically grounded discipline.' IMPORTANT ATTRIBUTION: the formal theorems the survey leans on are NOT the authors' own results -- the coverage-separation theorem is Song et al. [16], the overoptimization scaling law is Gao et al. [11] (and Rafailov et al. [19] for DPO), and the preference-collapse theorem is Xiao et al. [21]. The PsiPO objective itself is Azar et al. [7]. The authors' novelty is the synthesis and cross-mapping, not the individual theorems.
threat_model: This is an AI-safety/alignment survey rather than an adversarial-security paper, so there is no external attacker in the classic sense; the paper does not posit one and I do not invent one. The safety concern it does address (and which the authors themselves foreground) is optimization pathology: a learned reward model or implicit reward is a 'proxy' that, when optimized, can be exploited in a Goodhart's-law manner so that true objective quality declines even as proxy reward rises (reward overoptimization / reward hacking). Related failure modes framed as safety risks: length hacking (models game annotator/reward-model length bias to inflate verbosity), mode collapse and preference collapse (over-strong or absent regularization collapses the policy onto a single high-reward mode, disproportionately erasing minority/underrepresented preferences per Xiao et al. [21]), and likelihood displacement (DPO's gradient asymmetry drives dispreferred-response likelihood down faster than preferred likelihood up, harming nearby capabilities). These are self-inflicted / specification-gaming risks, not attacks.
limitations: As a survey its evidentiary weight is borrowed, not generated: every quantitative and formal claim traces to a cited paper, so the contribution is organizational and cannot be independently verified from this document. The empirical Table 3 aggregates numbers reported across heterogeneous sources with differing base models, training recipes, and evaluation protocols, so cross-method comparisons within the table are not controlled apples-to-apples results and should not be read as a benchmark the authors ran. Several table cells are incomplete (GSM8K blank for the Gemma-2-9B-it rows). The paper is by two independent researchers, non-peer-reviewed (arXiv, Jan 2026). It offers no new algorithm, ablation, or reproduction, and the strong framing ('empirical art into a theoretically grounded discipline') outstrips what a synthesis can establish. Open problems the authors themselves flag as unresolved: scaling laws for alignment ('a Chinchilla for alignment'), listwise/partial-ranking feedback, multi-objective/Pareto alignment, distribution shift and continual alignment, and the theory of in-context alignment.

datasets_used: No original datasets -- survey with no experiments. Benchmarks referenced from cited literature: AlpacaEval 2 (length-controlled), MT-Bench, Arena-Hard, GSM8K, HH-RLHF
quantitative_results: Table 3 (synthesized from literature, NOT authors' own experiments) AlpacaEval 2 LC on Llama-3-8B: SFT 15.2%, PPO 28.4%, DPO 25.1%, IPO 24.8%, KTO 23.6%, SimPO 31.5%, ORPO 26.3%
Table 3 MT-Bench on Llama-3-8B: SFT 7.1, PPO 7.8, DPO 7.6, IPO 7.5, KTO 7.4, SimPO 7.9, ORPO 7.6
Table 3 Arena-Hard on Llama-3-8B: SFT 18.3%, PPO 31.2%, DPO 27.8%, IPO 26.9%, KTO 25.4%, SimPO 34.1%, ORPO 28.5%
Table 3 GSM8K on Llama-3-8B: SFT 72.1%, PPO 74.3%, DPO 73.2%, IPO 73.0%, KTO 72.8%, SimPO 73.9%, ORPO 73.1%
Table 3 relative training cost: ORPO 1.0x, SimPO 1.2x, KTO 1.3x, DPO/IPO 1.5x, PPO 4-8x
Table 3 on Gemma-2-9B-it: SimPO AlpacaEval 2 LC 72.4% / MT-Bench 8.5 / Arena-Hard 59.1%; WPO 76.7% / 8.6 / 62.3% (GSM8K not reported)
Data-quality-dominates finding (Ivison et al. [18], cited): ~8% gain from better preference data vs. ~2.5% gain from PPO over DPO
Overoptimization scaling law (Gao et al. [11], cited): R_gold = d0 + d1*sqrt(KL) - d2*KL under RL; R_gold = d0 + d1*sqrt(d3*log n) - d2*d3*log n under best-of-n
